01a_quotes.title: The Innovation Tax: Generative AI Adoption, Productivity Paradox, and Systemic Risk in the U.S. Banking Sector

study_design: Using a novel dataset linking SEC 10-Q filings to Federal Reserve regulatory data for 809 financial institutions over 2018–2025, we employ two complementary identification strategies: Dynamic Spatial Durbin Models (DSDM) to capture network spillovers and Synthetic Difference-in-Differences (SDID) for causal inference using the November 2022 ChatGPT release as an exogenous shock.
To quantify network spillovers and test Hypotheses 1 and 4, we estimate a Dynamic Spatial Durbin Model following Elhorst (2014) and LeSage & Pace (2009).
To identify the causal effect of AI adoption and test Hypotheses 2 and 3, we employ the Synthetic Difference-in-Differences framework developed by Arkhangelsky et al. (2021).
Our identification strategy exploits the November 2022 public release of ChatGPT as a plausibly exogenous shock to the banking industry's AI production function.
We define the treatment period as 2023Q1, when the "Generative AI" revolution became salient to financial institutions.
We estimate standard errors using the placebo-based bootstrap procedure recommended by Arkhangelsky et al. (2021).
We report results based on B= 200 bootstrap replications.
task: We identify GenAI adoption through systematic text analysis of SEC filings.
For each filing, we count keyword occurrences and construct two measures: GenAI Mentions and a binary treatment indicator.
population: Our initial sample comprises 809 unique bank holding companies.

After requiring non-missing values for key variables and a minimum of four quarterly observations, our estimation sample includes 126 banks with complete data, of which 41 are classified as AI adopters (having at least one quarter with positive GenAI mentions) and 85 serve as control banks (zero GenAI mentions throughout the sample period).
The sample covers 809 unique bank holding companies observed quarterly from 2018Q1 to 2025Q4.
arms: Banks that mention GenAI in their SEC filings after 2023Q1 are "treated," while banks that never mention GenAI serve as controls.
41 are classified as AI adopters (having at least one quarter with positive GenAI mentions) and 85 serve as control banks (zero GenAI mentions throughout the sample period).
Large banks are defined as those in the top quartile of average total assets; small banks are in the bottom three quartiles.
Treated Banks 10, Control Banks 36 in the full sample; Treated Banks 4, Control Banks 8 for large banks; Treated Banks 6, Control Banks 28 for small banks.
outcome_definitions: ROA (Return on Assets): Net income divided by total assets for bank i in quarter t, expressed in percentage points. Winsorized at the 1st and 99th percentiles to mitigate outlier influence.
ROE (Return on Equity): Net income divided by total equity for bank i in quarter t, expressed in percentage points. Winsorized at the 1st and 99th percentiles.
Our primary productivity measures are ROA and ROE.
Both measures are winsorized at the 1st and 99th percentiles to mitigate the influence of outliers.
statistical_results: DSDM estimates show that AI-adopting banks are high performers (β >0)
The causal SDID analysis documents a significant "Implementation Tax"—adopting banks experience a 428-basis-point decline in ROE as they absorb GenAI integration costs.
Bottom-quartile banks suffering a 517-basis-point ROE decline compared to 129 basis points for larger banks
Our DSDM analysis reveals significant positive spillovers (θ= 0.161 for ROA, p <0.01; θ= 0.679 for ROE, p <0.05)
Spillovers among large banks reaching θ= 3.13 for ROE
The direct effect of AI adoption (β) is positive and statistically significant across specifications: 3.7 basis points for ROA and 42 basis points for ROE using the network weight matrix.
Using the network weight matrix, θ= 0.161 (p <0.01) for ROA and θ= 0.679 (p <0.05) for ROE.
Tier 1 capital ratios are positively associated with ROA (0.087, p <0.01)
The digitalization index shows divergent effects: positive for ROA (0.099, p <0.01) but negative for ROE (−1.71, p <0.01)
CEO age exhibits a marginally negative effect on ROE (−0.48, p <0.10)
AI adoption (β) 0.0373** for ROA and 0.4199*** for ROE using Wnetwork
W×AI Adoption (θ) 0.1606*** for ROA and 0.6787** for ROE using Wnetwork
Temporal Lag (τ) 0.6363*** for ROA and 0.6967*** for ROE using Wnetwork
Spatial Lag (ρ) 0.6166*** for ROA and 0.7453*** for ROE using Wnetwork
Spatial-Temporal Lag (η) −0.2898*** for ROA and −0.4653*** for ROE using Wnetwork
Direct Effect 0.0923** for ROA and 0.7634*** for ROE
Indirect Effect (Spillovers) 0.4289*** for ROA and 3.8941*** for ROE
Total Effect 0.5212*** for ROA and 4.6575*** for ROE
Indirect/Total Ratio 82.3% for ROA and 83.6% for ROE
AI adoption causes ROA to decline by 46 basis points and ROE to decline by 428 basis points.
ATT −0.456** for ROA in full sample
ATT −4.282*** for ROE in full sample
Large Banks: ATT −0.159*** for ROA and −1.286*** for ROE
Small Banks: ATT −0.594** for ROA and −5.167*** for ROE
Smaller banks (bottom 75% by assets) experience a substantially larger Implementation Tax: an ROE decline of 517 basis points compared to only 129 basis points for larger banks.
Smaller banks suffer a 59-basis-point ROA decline versus 16 basis points for larger institutions.
In Panel (a), the ATT on ROA improves from approximately −0.10% in the quarter immediately preceding adoption to +0.30% in the adoption quarter.
This 40-basis-point improvement is economically substantial and statistically significant.

Panel (b) reveals a more complex pattern for ROE. The pre-treatment decline is more pronounced (reaching −3.1% at t=−1), reflecting the leverage-amplified impact of transition costs on equity returns.
Following adoption, ROE improves substantially, representing a 190-basis-point improvement relative to the pre-treatment trend.
For large banks, the spillover coefficient θ is massive: 0.35 for ROA and an extraordinary 3.13 for ROE.
For small banks, spillovers are modest (0.11 for ROA) or statistically insignificant (0.15 for ROE).

W×AI Adoption (θ) 0.1055** for ROA and 0.1502 for ROE for small banks

Spatial Lag (ρ) 0.5999*** for ROA and 0.7522*** for ROE for small banks

ambiguity_or_caveats: While the DSDM effectively captures network spillovers and cross-sectional associations, it faces a fundamental endogeneity concern: banks may adopt AI because they are already becoming more productive, rather than AI causing productivity improvements.

The positive β coefficient may reflect a causal effect of AI on productivity, positive selection whereby high-productivity "frontier" banks are more likely to adopt AI, or reverse causality in which improving productivity frees resources for AI investment.
The DSDM cannot distinguish between these interpretations.
Our treatment measure relies on disclosed AI adoption in SEC filings, which may understate actual implementation.
Our sample period captures only the initial phase of GenAI diffusion; longer-term effects as the J-Curve completes may differ substantially.
Our network analysis is based on asset similarity rather than actual AI vendor relationships; richer data on technology infrastructure would enable more precise spillover estimation.
The key identifying assumption is that, absent the ChatGPT shock, treated and control banks would have followed similar productivity trajectories.
SDID does not require parallel trends. Instead, it re-weights the control group to match the treated group's pre-treatment trajectory perfectly.
SDID is consistent if either the parallel trends assumption holds (making standard DiD valid) or the synthetic control weights perfectly match pre-treatment outcomes (making synthetic control valid).

While not directly observable in our data, industry reports suggest that AI-adopting banks rely heavily on similar LLM architectures (GPT-4, Claude, Gemini) and vendor solutions, creating shared technical failure modes.

01a_quotes.notes: This is an empirical research paper with original experiments. The paper combines two complementary econometric approaches (DSDM and SDID) to study the causal impact of generative AI adoption on bank productivity and systemic risk. The main findings include: (1) a productivity paradox where AI-adopting banks show positive cross-sectional associations (DSDM) but negative causal effects (SDID); (2) heterogeneous implementation costs by bank size, with smaller banks experiencing larger ROE declines; (3) significant positive network spillovers, particularly among large banks (θ=3.13 for ROE), indicating algorithmic coupling and systemic synchronization risk. The paper uses the November 2022 ChatGPT release as an exogenous shock to identify causal effects. Results are presented across multiple specifications and robustness checks.

01_extraction.title: The Innovation Tax: Generative AI Adoption, Productivity Paradox, and Systemic Risk in the U.S. Banking Sector
01_extraction.study_design_description: Using a novel dataset linking SEC 10-Q filings to Federal Reserve regulatory data for 809 financial institutions over 2018–2025, the study employs two complementary identification strategies: Dynamic Spatial Durbin Models (DSDM) to capture network spillovers and Synthetic Difference-in-Differences (SDID) for causal inference using the November 2022 ChatGPT release as an exogenous shock. The treatment period is defined as 2023Q1, when the "Generative AI" revolution became salient to financial institutions. Standard errors are estimated using the placebo-based bootstrap procedure with B=200 bootstrap replications.
01_extraction.primary_task_description: GenAI adoption is identified through systematic text analysis of SEC filings. For each filing, keyword occurrences are counted and two measures are constructed: GenAI Mentions and a binary treatment indicator.
01_extraction.population_description: The initial sample comprises 809 unique bank holding companies observed quarterly from 2018Q1 to 2025Q4. After requiring non-missing values for key variables and a minimum of four quarterly observations, the estimation sample includes 126 banks with complete data, of which 41 are classified as AI adopters (having at least one quarter with positive GenAI mentions) and 85 serve as control banks (zero GenAI mentions throughout the sample period).

arms.description: Banks that mention GenAI in their SEC filings after 2023Q1; classified as AI adopters having at least one quarter with positive GenAI mentions.
Banks that never mention GenAI throughout the sample period; serve as controls.

outcomes.outcome_description: Return on Assets: Net income divided by total assets for bank i in quarter t, expressed in percentage points. Winsorized at the 1st and 99th percentiles to mitigate outlier influence.
Return on Equity: Net income divided by total equity for bank i in quarter t, expressed in percentage points. Winsorized at the 1st and 99th percentiles.

01_extraction.notes: This paper combines Dynamic Spatial Durbin Models (DSDM) and Synthetic Difference-in-Differences (SDID) to study AI adoption in banking. The DSDM captures network spillovers and cross-sectional associations but faces endogeneity concerns: the positive β coefficient may reflect causal effects, positive selection of high-productivity banks, or reverse causality. The SDID analysis, which uses the November 2022 ChatGPT release as an exogenous shock, documents a significant "Implementation Tax" with heterogeneous effects by bank size. Smaller banks experience substantially larger ROE declines (517 basis points) compared to larger banks (129 basis points). The paper reports both direct effects and network spillovers, with large banks showing massive spillover coefficients (θ=3.13 for ROE). Treatment measure relies on disclosed AI adoption in SEC filings, which may understate actual implementation. Sample period captures only initial phase of GenAI diffusion; longer-term effects may differ. Network analysis based on asset similarity rather than actual AI vendor relationships. Significance levels indicated by asterisks: ** p<0.05, *** p<0.01, marginal p<0.10.

02_method.rationale: The study uses the November 2022 ChatGPT release as an exogenous shock and employs Synthetic Difference-in-Differences (SDID) for causal inference, with treatment period defined as 2023Q1. This is a quasi-experimental design exploiting a natural event rather than random assignment, comparing AI adopters to non-adopters using advanced identification strategies.

per_outcome.operationalization: Net income divided by total assets, winsorized at 1st and 99th percentiles, expressed in percentage points
Net income divided by total equity, winsorized at 1st and 99th percentiles, expressed in percentage points

04_onet.rationale: The study participants are researchers conducting systematic text analysis of SEC filings, counting keyword occurrences, and constructing quantitative measures from unstructured data. This is quintessential data analysis work. The verbatim task description states they perform 'systematic text analysis' and construct measures through data processing. This maps clearly to the generalized work activity of analyzing data rather than any specific occupation.

llm_extracted.ai_arm_selection_rationale: The paper compares banks that adopted GenAI (identified through SEC filings mentioning GenAI keywords after November 2022 ChatGPT release) versus banks that never mentioned GenAI. This is the primary treatment contrast throughout the paper.
llm_extracted.notes: The paper uses SDID (Synthetic Difference-in-Differences) to estimate causal effects. Table 6 reports the Average Treatment Effect on the Treated (ATT) for ROE as -4.282*** (SE=0.887) percentage points. This is the causal effect of AI adoption on Return on Equity. The outcome is in levels (percentage points), not log-transformed. Table 3 provides the baseline mean ROE of 10.815% across the full sample. The sample sizes come from the notes to Table 6: 10 treated banks and 36 control banks in the full sample analysis. However, the paper states the estimation sample includes 126 banks total with 41 AI adopters and 85 controls (page 23). Using the larger sample counts as they represent the full analytical sample. The negative ATT indicates AI adoption causes ROE to decline by 428 basis points during the implementation phase (the 'Implementation Tax'). This is a productivity measure where larger ROE values are better, so the negative effect indicates worse performance.
source_quotes: Table 6: Synthetic Difference-in-Differences Estimates: The Implementation Tax ... Panel B: Return on Equity (ROE, %) ... ATT −4.282*** (0.887)

our estimation sample includes 126 banks with complete data, of which 41 are classified as AI adopters (having at least one quarter with positive GenAI mentions) and 85 serve as control banks (zero GenAI mentions throughout the sample period)
The aggregate estimates in Column (1) reveal the 'Implementation Tax': AI adoption causes ROA to decline by 46 basis points and ROE to decline by 428 basis points.

computed.notes: The paper uses SDID (Synthetic Difference-in-Differences) to estimate causal effects. Table 6 reports the Average Treatment Effect on the Treated (ATT) for ROE as -4.282*** (SE=0.887) percentage points. This is the causal effect of AI adoption on Return on Equity. The outcome is in levels (percentage points), not log-transformed. Table 3 provides the baseline mean ROE of 10.815% across the full sample. The sample sizes come from the notes to Table 6: 10 treated banks and 36 control banks in the full sample analysis. However, the paper states the estimation sample includes 126 banks total with 41 AI adopters and 85 controls (page 23). Using the larger sample counts as they represent the full analytical sample. The negative ATT indicates AI adoption causes ROE to decline by 428 basis points during the implementation phase (the 'Implementation Tax'). This is a productivity measure where larger ROE values are better, so the negative effect indicates worse performance.

llm_extracted.ai_arm_selection_rationale: This paper does not report a controlled experiment comparing human-only vs. human+AI performance on a quality outcome. The study is a descriptive empirical analysis of GenAI adoption in the U.S. banking sector using observational data (SEC filings + Federal Reserve regulatory data). The 'treatment' is AI adoption (measured by keyword mentions in filings), and the outcomes are productivity metrics (ROA, ROE) at the bank level. There are no experimental arms, no baseline human task performance, and no controlled AI intervention. The DSDM and SDID estimates compare AI-adopting banks to non-adopting banks in an observational setting, not a randomized or controlled experiment with individual worker or task-level quality measurements. The paper explicitly states it examines 'causal impact of Generative Artificial Intelligence (GenAI) adoption on productivity and systemic risk' at the institutional level, not worker-level quality outcomes. No speed or quality metrics (in the sense of task completion time or task output quality) are reported. The outcomes are financial performance ratios (ROA, ROE) which are not quality outcomes in the schema's intended sense (accuracy, error rate, rubric score, etc.). Therefore, this paper does not fit the extraction schema's requirements.
llm_extracted.notes: After re-reading the full PDF, I confirm that this is an observational study of bank-level productivity (ROA, ROE) following GenAI adoption, not an experiment measuring human task performance with and without AI assistance. The paper uses econometric methods (DSDM, SDID) to estimate causal effects of institutional AI adoption on financial performance, but does not report individual-level quality outcomes, task completion metrics, or experimental arms. The 'arms' in the prior context refer to banks classified by adoption status (AI-adopting vs. non-adopting), not experimental conditions. No means, SDs, or effect sizes for a quality outcome (accuracy, error rate, rubric score) are present. The paper is not resolvable under the schema.
source_quotes: This paper evaluates the causal impact of Generative Artificial Intelligence (GenAI) adoption on productivity and systemic risk in the U.S. banking sector.

Using a novel dataset linking SEC 10-Q filings to Federal Reserve regulatory data for 809 financial institutions over 2018–2025, we employ two complementary identification strategies: Dynamic Spatial Durbin Models (DSDM) to capture network spillovers and Synthetic Difference-in-Differences (SDID) for causal inference using the November 2022 ChatGPT release as an exogenous shock.
Our primary productivity measures are: ROAit = Net Incomeit / Total Assetsit × 100; ROEit = Net Incomeit / Total Equityit × 100
The treatment indicator D^GenAI_it equals one if bank i mentions any GenAI-related keyword in quarter t's filing.

computed.notes: After re-reading the full PDF, I confirm that this is an observational study of bank-level productivity (ROA, ROE) following GenAI adoption, not an experiment measuring human task performance with and without AI assistance. The paper uses econometric methods (DSDM, SDID) to estimate causal effects of institutional AI adoption on financial performance, but does not report individual-level quality outcomes, task completion metrics, or experimental arms. The 'arms' in the prior context refer to banks classified by adoption status (AI-adopting vs. non-adopting), not experimental conditions. No means, SDs, or effect sizes for a quality outcome (accuracy, error rate, rubric score) are present. The paper is not resolvable under the schema. | arm pair unresolvable
